result:
I’ve been thinking about the way light moves through rooms at different hours—how at dawn it’s almost shy, pooling in corners like it’s testing the temperature of the wood floor before it commits. By late morning it turns brash, shoving itself into every drawer and picture frame, exposing dust we didn’t invite to the party. Then late afternoon comes in with that honey-slanted apology, forgiving the glare of noon, gilding everything just long enough for us to believe the world might actually keep its promises.

Sometimes I catch myself trying to bottle that light—holding my phone up at weird angles, twisting the blinds open and shut like I’m tuning a cosmic radio, as if I could JPEG a feeling. But the file always falls short; the pixels don’t remember the warmth on my forearms or the quiet creak the house makes when the sun hits the south-facing beam that’s been sagging since ’73. The photo becomes a postcard from a country I can’t re-enter, because the visa stamped in my palm is the moment itself, and it expires the instant I reach for it.

I wonder if that’s why we get so attached to ordinary objects: the chipped mug, the crooked lampshade, the squeaky third stair. They’re the reluctant witnesses to our private light shows. When the bulb burns out or the mug cracks in half, it feels like a small betrayal, like someone burned the only map that showed the way back to Saturday mornings when the kids still believed cereal could be a banquet and the biggest decision was whether to watch cartoons in the living room or the basement.

I keep a broken pocket watch in my desk drawer—my grandfather’s. It stopped at 8:47 sometime in 1987. I used to think I should get it repaired, but that would erase the exact second the universe shrugged and said “enough.” Now I wind it anyway, just until the tension in the spring feels like a heartbeat, then let it rest. Every click is a small rebellion against the digital tyranny of my phone, where time is a liquid crystal panopticon that never blinks, never forgives.

Tonight, the streetlights are flickering in a Morse code I almost remember from childhood summers when we’d sneak out with flashlights and talk to the dark in secret patterns only nine-year-olds consider sophisticated. I want to answer them, but the syntax is buried under mortgages and medications and all the other words that get heavier as they age. So I just stand at the window, palm against the cool glass, watching the orange pulses trade gossip with the moon, while somewhere inside me a pocket watch ticks itself hoarse trying to reach 8:48.
